coredata.dc:title: Selenium-doped carbon dots inhibit ferroptosis by multi-hierarchy iron chelation and mitochondrial homeostasis regulation to control inflammation

coredata.dc:description: Ferroptosis has been identified as a prevalent pathological process in both infectious and non-infectious inflammation. Multiregional iron accumulation within extracellular, cytoplasmic, and subcellular compartments, particularly into mitochondria, can trigger lipid peroxidation and subsequent ferroptosis. Meanwhile, mitochondrial dysfunction, as a hallmark feature of ferroptosis, also exacerbates ferroptosis and inflammation. Here, a strategy of multi-hierarchy iron chelation coupled with mitochondrial homeostasis regulation is proposed to inhibit ferroptosis and relieve inflammation based on selenium-doped carbon dots (SeCDs). SeCDs with reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging capacity can be efficiently internalized into cells and enriched in mitochondria. Through the multi-hierarchy chelation for extracellular, cytoplasmic, and mitochondrial iron, iron overload is remarkably inhibited. Meanwhile, promoting glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) expression and activating the AMPK pathway can restrain the metabolism of glycerophospholipids and polyunsaturated fatty acids and lipid peroxidation. Importantly, SeCDs restore mitochondrial homeostasis by chelating mitochondrial iron, scavenging mtROS, and increasing the level of GPX4, thereby enhancing ferroptosis suppression. Further, SeCDs rescue the osteogenic differentiation potential of stem cells, which is generally weakened by ferroptosis. In vivo experiments prove that SeCDs can significantly inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation, and facilitate tissue regeneration. Thus, this multifunctional nanosystem provides a promising therapeutic strategy to inhibit ferroptosis and ease inflammation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of SeCDs. (A) The synthesis process of SeCDs. (B, C) TEM and HRTEM image of SeCDs. (D) Size-distribution histograms of SeCDs with superimposed Gaussian fits. (E) FTIR spectra of curcumin, L-selenocystine, and SeCDs. (F) UV–Vis spectrum of SeCDs. (G) Fluorescence spectra of SeCDs excited at different wavelengths. (H-K) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Se 3d. (L) Chelating ability of SeCDs to different metal ions. (M) SEM image and element mapping analysis of SeCDs/Fe3+ complex.

Antioxidant properties and biocompatibility of SeCDs in vitro. (A) Absorption spectra of TMB after reacting with •OH at different concentrations of SeCDs. (B) •OH scavenging ability of SeCDs. (C) Absorbance spectra of SeCDs at different concentrations in DPPH solution. (D) RNS scavenging ability of SeCDs. Cell viability of (E) GFs and (F) PDLSCs incubated with SeCDs for 24 h. (G) Live/Dead staining of GFs cultured with 5 μg/mL of SeCDs (SeCDs-5), 10 μg/mL of SeCDs (SeCDs-10), and 20 μg/mL of SeCDs (SeCDs-20) for 24 h. (H) Intracellular ROS levels in GFs. (I) Relative fluorescence intensity of GFs stained by DCFH-DA. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Intracellular localization of SeCDs and iron chelation ability. (A) Schematic illustration of ferroptosis caused by iron overload in an inflammatory environment. (B) Lysosomes stained with LysoTracker Green. (C) Mitochondria stained with Mito-tracker Red. (D-E) Fluorescent changes of SeCDs co-localized with organelles. (F) Fluorescence intensity detection at Ex/Em = 543/580. (G) FerroOrange staining of GFs treated with SeCDs and FAC. (H) Detection of MDA levels of GFs. (I-K) Quantitative analysis of the mRNA expression of the ferroptosis-related genes. (L) Mito-ferroGreen staining of GFs treated with SeCDs and FAC. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not significant. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SeCDs inhibition of LPS-induced ferroptosis. (A) Confocal fluorescence images of FerroOrange staining of GFs. (B) Representative fluorescence images of C11/BODIPY 581/591 staining and (C) quantitative analysis. (D) The mRNA expression of the ferroptosis-related genes at 12 h. (E) Immunofluorescence staining images of GPX4. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not significant.

SeCDs restoration of mitochondrial homeostasis. (A) MitoSox-Red staining of GFs. (B-C) Fluorescence images of MMP using JC-1 after different treatments and quantitative analysis. (D-E) Quantitative analysis of mPTP and fluorescence staining. (F) TEM images of GFs after various treatments. (G) Detection of intracellular ATP levels of GFs. (H) Quantitative analysis of GSH/GSSG. (I) Mito-tracker and Mito-FerroGreen staining at a subcellular level. (J) Schematic diagram of the mechanism by which SeCDs protected mitochondria and inhibited ferroptosis. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SeCDs inhibition of inflammation and promotion of osteogenic differentiation. (A) qRT-PCR analysis of inflammatory factors of GFs. (B) Immunofluorescence staining images of the IL-6. (C-D) Relative mRNA expression of the osteogenic marker genes and ALP staining of PDLSCs at 7 d. (E) Immunofluorescent staining of OCN at 7 d. (F) ARS staining of PDLSCs at 14 d. The error bars indicated mean ± standard deviations. **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Mechanistic analysis of SeCDs on ferroptosis inhibition. A) Bubble map of differential expression of lipid metabolites classes. B) Heatmap of differential expression of lipid metabolites. C) Top 10 upregulated and downregulated metabolites with the highest VIP values. D) Pearson correlation analysis of different lipid metabolites. E) Enriched KEGG pathway analysis for the identified differentially expressed lipid metabolite. F-H) Arachidonic acid metabolism, linoleic acid metabolism, glycerophospholipid metabolism based on GSEA. I) Protein expression of AMPK, p-AMPK, ACC and p-ACC. J) Schematic diagram of ferroptosis regulation by SeCDs. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001.

Inhibitory effect of SeCDs on ferroptosis and inflammation in periodontal tissues. (A) Schematic diagram of establishing the P.gingivalis LPS-induced periodontitis model. (B) Immunohistochemistry staining of GPX4 and SLC7A11 and (C) semi-quantification of positively stained cells. (D) Immunofluorescence images of GPX4 and (E) SLC7A11 from different groups. (F) Immunohistochemical images of IL-6 from different groups. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001.

The therapeutic effect of SeCDs on periodontal bone regeneration. (A) 3D reconstructed images of the maxillary molars by Micro-CT. Red lines represented the alveolar bone crest (ABC) and the cementoenamel junction (CEJ). (B) The quantitative statistics of different parameters of the alveolar bone by reconstruction. (C) H&E and (D) Masson staining images of the periodontal tissues after 4 weeks of treatment. (E) Immunohistochemical images of Runx2. The error bars indicate mean ± standard deviations. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of SeCDs-mediated multi-hierarchy iron chelation and mitochondrial homeostasis regulation strategy to inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation.

head.ce:title: Selenium-doped carbon dots inhibit ferroptosis by multi-hierarchy iron chelation and mitochondrial homeostasis regulation to control inflammation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ferroptosis has been identified as a prevalent pathological process in both infectious and non-infectious inflammation. Multiregional iron accumulation within extracellular, cytoplasmic, and subcellular compartments, particularly into mitochondria, can trigger lipid peroxidation and subsequent ferroptosis. Meanwhile, mitochondrial dysfunction, as a hallmark feature of ferroptosis, also exacerbates ferroptosis and inflammation. Here, a strategy of multi-hierarchy iron chelation coupled with mitochondrial homeostasis regulation is proposed to inhibit ferroptosis and relieve inflammation based on selenium-doped carbon dots (SeCDs). SeCDs with reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging capacity can be efficiently internalized into cells and enriched in mitochondria. Through the multi-hierarchy chelation for extracellular, cytoplasmic, and mitochondrial iron, iron overload is remarkably inhibited. Meanwhile, promoting glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) expression and activating the AMPK pathway can restrain the metabolism of glycerophospholipids and polyunsaturated fatty acids and lipid peroxidation. Importantly, SeCDs restore mitochondrial homeostasis by chelating mitochondrial iron, scavenging mtROS, and increasing the level of GPX4, thereby enhancing ferroptosis suppression. Further, SeCDs rescue the osteogenic differentiation potential of stem cells, which is generally weakened by ferroptosis. In vivo experiments prove that SeCDs can significantly inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation, and facilitate tissue regeneration. Thus, this multifunctional nanosystem provides a promising therapeutic strategy to inhibit ferroptosis and ease inflammation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammation, recognized as a key pathological process underlying numerous diseases, has long been considered a persistent medical challenge [1]. Recently, ferroptosis, as a newly discovered form of cell death caused by excessive accumulation of iron-dependent lipid peroxidation, has been proven to be intimately associated with the occurrence and progression of inflammatory diseases (e.g. periodontitis [2], neurological diseases [3], acute kidney injury [4,5], and ischemia/reperfusion [6]). Ferroptosis can participate in inflammatory reactions on one hand through lipoxygenases and cyclooxygenases [7]. On the other hand, it exacerbates the inflammatory process by triggering the innate immune system and releasing inflammatory mediators such as interleukin-6 and interleukin-8 [8]. Furthermore, it also suppresses the osteogenic differentiation of stem cells by influencing the dynamic equilibrium between osteoblasts and osteoclasts as well as the microenvironment for bone regeneration [9,10]. Imbalanced iron metabolism, disrupted lipid metabolism, and elevated levels of lipid reactive oxygen species (ROS) serve as common features bridging ferroptosis and inflammation, while ferroptosis inhibition exerts anti-inflammatory effects and mitigates damage in several inflammatory diseases [11,12]. Therefore, the concept of targeting ferroptosis as a therapeutic strategy for inflammatory diseases is regarded as a promising approach.

Iron homeostasis is indispensable for maintaining a diverse range of cellular functions, including metabolic processes, electron transfer [13], energy synthesis [14], and DNA synthesis [15]. However, iron overload has become a driving factor for many diseases. Clinical investigations have revealed that during infectious inflammation, the elevated iron concentration within the microenvironment is ascribable to hemolysis induced by pathogens [16]. Iron ions can participate in the damage process of biological macromolecules by inducing oxidative stress, and in turn, oxidative stress decompensation can intensify Fe3+ binding to transferrin, further promoting iron accumulation and triggering inflammation [17]. Apart from the occurrence of iron accumulation in the extracellular inflammatory microenvironment, bacterial products such as lipopolysaccharide (LPS) also enhance the cellular uptake of Fe3+, which are transported intracellularly and reduced to Fe2+ under the action of the six-transmembrane epithelial antigen of prostate 3 enzymes (STEAP3) [18]. Excessive Fe2+ within cells set off the Fenton reaction, which leads to the oxidation of macromolecules, particularly lipid molecules like polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs). This promotes the accumulation of metabolites including malondialdehyde (MDA) and 4-hydroxynonenal (4-HNE), ultimately triggering ferroptosis and intensifying inflammation [19]. Therefore, synchronously chelating extracellular and intracellular iron and reducing lipid peroxidation would inhibit ferroptosis efficiently.

Mitochondria, as the primary hub for iron metabolism, utilization, and storage, perform crucial functions in heme synthesis and energy conversion processes [20,21]. It has been demonstrated that mitochondrial dysfunction exerts a profound impact on ferroptosis, while an excessive iron content can cause ferroptosis-associated mitochondrial damage. Cytoplasmic iron is transported into mitochondria via three pathways: VDAC2/3, Mfrn1/Mfrn2, and the mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) along with Ca2+ transporters [22]. Mitochondrial iron overload leads to elevated levels of mitochondrial ROS (mtROS) through the Fenton reaction, activates mitochondrial NOX4 and 15-lipoxygenase, and thereby results in phospholipid peroxidation and iron-dependent cell death [23]. Additionally, the expression of the “executioner molecule” GPX4 is decreased in the process of ferroptosis [24,25]. GPX4, a selenoprotein that is precisely localized in both the cytoplasm and mitochondria, possesses the remarkable capability of catalyzing the specific conversion of lipid peroxides to lipid alcohols via utilizing glutathione, thereby protecting both mitochondria and other cytoplasmic contents from oxidative damage [26]. The supplement of Se could promote the synthesis of endogenous antioxidant enzymes [27], and aid in the restoration of damaged mitochondrial functions [28]. Therefore, relieving mitochondrial iron overload, enhancing GPX4 expression, and restoring mitochondrial homeostasis could further inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation.

Here, a strategy of multi-hierarchy iron chelation and mitochondrial homeostasis regulation is proposed, which could overcome the limitations of simple iron chelation and achieve inhibition of ferroptosis and inflammation (Scheme 1 ). Se-doped carbon dots (SeCDs) with iron chelation function and ROS scavenging ability are prepared by the one-step method. SeCDs can chelate extracellular/intracellular/mitochondrial iron ions to remarkably relieve iron overload and synergistically activate the AMPK signaling pathway to inhibit ferroptosis. Meanwhile, by enhancing the biosynthesis of GPX4, elevating mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), reversing the excessive opening of mPTP, and inhibiting mtROS generation, SeCDs are capable of efficiently restoring mitochondrial homeostasis and functions, following further to alleviate ferroptosis and fight inflammation. Interestingly, SeCDs also can restore stem cell differentiation inhibited by iron overload. By multi-hierarchy iron chelation and mitochondrial homeostasis regulation-mediated ferroptosis inhibition and differentiation potential restoration, SeCDs remarkably restrain inflammation and propel the regeneration process in vivo.

Selenocystine, curcumin, and citric acid were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Gibco, Waltham, MA, USA) and fetal bovine serum were obtained from Gibco (FBS, Grand Island, NY, USA). Ferrostatin-1 was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8), alkaline phosphatase (ALP) staining kits, DCFH-DA fluorescent probe and Alizarin red S (ARS) staining kits were bought from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Lipid Peroxidation Probe C11 BODIPY 581/591, Mito-FerroGreen and FerroOrange probe were obtained from Dojindo (Kyushu, Japan).

SeCDs were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 16.7 mg of selenocystine in an alkaline solution and 18.4 mg of curcumin in ultrapure water containing 200 mg of citric acid were mixed and diluted to 30 mL. Then, they were transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave reactor and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After that, the reacted solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was dialyzed with deionized water and replaced every 12 h for a duration of 2 d.

The morphology of SeCDs was observed using a field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2, Thermo Fisher Scientific, US) and a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Pro G5, Phenome). Fourier transform infrared spectra within the range of 400–4000 cm−1 were collected on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5) using the KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, ESCALAB250Xi, United States) was used for collecting element information. The absorption spectra of SeCDs were measured by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Lambda 365, PerkinElmer, USA), and the fluorescence spectra of SeCDs were obtained by fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi, F-4600, Tokyo, Japan) in a standard quartz cuvette.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SeCDs chelating metal ability and fluorescence stability
ce:section.ce:para: SeCDs (5 mg/mL) were fully mixed with 250 µM metal salt solution (MgCl2, CaCl2, MnCl2, FeSO4, and FeCl3) and stirred for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was monitored, and SEM equipped with energy-dispersive spectroscopy was used to observe the morphology and elemental composition of the resulting precipitate. SeCDs were mixed with different doses of 500 µM Fe3+ solution (1 µL, 2 µL and 4 µL), and the fluorescence intensity was measured after dilution. Performed SEM detection on the morphology of SeCDs/Fe3+ composite (SeCDs, 10 mg/mL; FeCl3, 1 M) and conducted XPS analysis. The SeCDs were added to the different pH solutions (5, 6, 7.4, 8) and incubated for 30 min to observe the change in fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of ROS scavenging activities of SeCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 0.1 mM 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl radical (DPPH) (0.7 mL) prepared in ethanol was combined with varying concentrations of SeCDs (0.1 mL, 10 µg/mL, 20 µg/mL, 50 µg/mL, 100 µg/mL). After incubation at room temperature in the dark for 30 min, the absorbance was measured and the DPPH scavenging rate was calculated. 12 mg of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was dissolved in 5 mL of DMSO. 200 µL of TMB solution was mixed with 30 μL of 10 mM CuCl2 solution and diluted to 2 mL. Subsequently, 200 µL of the solution was obtained after SeCDs reacted with H2O2 and added to the aforementioned 2 mL system. Then, observed the color change and conducted detection using an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer.

Gingival fibroblasts (GFs) and periodontal ligament stem cells (PDLSCs) were extracted from clinical tooth extraction patients by our scientific research team, and approved by the Medical Ethical Committee of School of Stomatology, Shandong University (Protocol Number: 20240334). The cytotoxicity of SeCDs on GFs and PDLSCs was assessed using CCK-8 assay and Calcein-AM/PI Double Staining Kit. Cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 per well and incubated overnight. After 24 h of incubation, the culture medium containing 10 % CCK-8 solution was added and further incubated for 30 min. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm using a Bio-Rad microplate reader. For fluorescence detection of cell viability, the same experimental groups and treatments were employed. Cells were stained with Calcein Acetoxymethyl Ester (AM) and Propidium Iodide (PI), and observed under a fluorescence microscope. GFs were seeded at a density of 1.5 × 105 per well in 6-well plates. After the cells adhered, they were exposed to a culture medium containing 1 μM hydrogen peroxide and various concentrations of SeCDs. The cells were then washed three times with PBS and incubated in a serum-free culture medium containing 10 μM DCFH-DA for 30 min. Subsequently, the cells were washed again with PBS and observed under a fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular localization of SeCDs and iron overload-induced ferroptosis
ce:section.ce:para: GFs were inoculated in a laser confocal plate, and treated with 5 μg/mL of SeCDs for 12 h. After washing with PBS, the cells were stained using Mito-tracker Red and Lyso-tracker Green according to the manufacturer's instructions. 1 x 104 GFs were inoculated in 96-well plates, and cultured overnight with 50 μM ferric ammonium citrate (FAC), 5 μg/mL of SeCDs (SeCDs-5), and 10 μg/mL of SeCDs (SeCDs-10) for 12 h, washed 3 times with PBS, 100 μL of PBS solution containing FerroOrange were added to each well, and cultured at 37 °C for 30 min. The fluorescence intensity of samples (Ex:543 nm, Em:580 nm) was detected by a multifunctional enzyme marker. Concurrently, the cells were treated with 50 μM FAC and various concentrations of SeCDs for 12 h for gene expression detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lipid ROS and mitochondrial ROS measurements
ce:section.ce:para: GFs were seeded onto laser confocal dishes and treated with LPS and SeCDs for 12 h. Subsequently, staining was performed for 30 min following the instructions provided for C11 BODIPY 581/591 and MitoSox Red. Images were acquired using a laser confocal microscope, and statistical analysis was conducted using Image J.

Detection of mitochondrial permeability transition pore (mPTP) and mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP): Cells were seeded onto laser confocal dishes and treated with LPS and SeCDs for 12 h. Each well was supplemented with 1 mL Calcein AM staining solution, and incubated at 37°C in the dark for 30 min. After washing three times with PBS, an assay buffer was added for observation under laser confocal microscopy (Calcein AM exhibits green fluorescence, Ex/Em = 494/517 nm). For JC-1 detection, 1 mL of JC-1 staining working solution was added and thoroughly mixed. The cells were then incubated at 37 °C for 20 min. After removing the supernatant, cells were washed twice with JC-1 staining buffer, observed under laser confocal microscopy, and quantitatively analyzed using Image J.

GFs were cultured in 6 cm diameter dishes at a density of 1 × 106 cells and treated with LPS and SeCDs (10 μg/mL), incubated for 24 h, and the digested cells were collected and centrifuged to obtain cell precipitates, washed 3 times with PBS, and then fixed in darkness at 4 °C with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 2 h. After dehydration, embedding, and staining, the samples were observed using a transmission electron microscope with attention to mitochondrial cristae morphology and mitochondrial membrane structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of intracellular iron, mitochondrial iron, GSH/GSSG, and malondialdehyde (MDA)
ce:section.ce:para: After conditional intervention, GFs were co-incubated with the working solution of FerroOrange and Mito-FerroGreen. The changes of iron ions in the cytoplasm and mitochondria were observed under a fluorescence microscope. The cells were cultured in 6-well plates and treated with LPS and SeCDs for 12 h. Subsequently, the cells were washed once with PBS and harvested by centrifugation. Then, according to the detection kit, quantitatively analyzed the content changes of GSH and GSSG. In addition, the MDA level was detected using the Lipid Peroxidation MDA Assay Kit (Beyotime, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

GFs were plated in 6-well plates at a density of 2 × 105 per well and incubated with 10 μg/mL of LPS with or without SeCDs for 12 h. The cells were washed twice with PBS before RNA extraction using the TRIzol method. Then, 1 μg/mL of the extracted total RNA was reverse transcribed into cDNA. SYBR Premix Ex Taq II kit was then employed to confirm the expression levels of ferroptosis-related genes, inflammation-related genes, and osteogenic genes. The primer sequences are listed in Table S1.

PDLSCs were seeded into 6-well plates and cultured with a density of 2.5 × 105 cells per well. After 7 d of LPS and/or SeCDs treatment and osteogenic induction, different treated cells were collected and the expression of osteogenic genes was detected. The cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde, washed three times with PBS, and then stained with an ALP staining kit. After different treatments and osteogenic induction, ARS staining was performed at 14 d.

Following the procedure outlined previously, cells were subjected to immunofluorescence detection. They were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde at room temperature for 20 min, washed three times with PBS, and permeabilized with PBS containing 0.5 % Triton X-100 for 30 min. Non-specific binding sites were blocked using a PBS solution containing 3 % bovine serum albumin. Samples were then incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies against GPX4, SLC7A11, IL-6, and OCN sourced from rabbits. Subsequently, the samples were incubated for 1 h with Alexa Fluor 594-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG. Cell cytoskeletons were labeled for 10 min using FITC-conjugated phalloidin, and nuclei were counterstained for 5 min with DAPI. Finally, the samples were observed under a laser confocal microscope.

The non-targeted liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) lipidomics platform was employed to analyze the lipid metabolites in lipopolysaccharide and SeCDs-induced GFs. The analytical approach involved multivariate statistical analysis techniques, such as principal component analysis (PCA) and orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA). Differential lipid metabolites were selected based on OPLS-DA model variable importance for the projection (VIP) values > 1 and a significance level (P) < 0.05. The linear correlation between metabolites was measured using the Pearson correlation coefficient. Furthermore, to gain a deeper understanding of the changes in metabolic pathways in different samples, differential metabolites were subjected to metabolic pathway enrichment analysis based on the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database and gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA).

GFs were cultured in 6-well plates, treated with lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and SeCDs for 12 h, and the total protein was extracted using RIPA lysis buffer containing protease and phosphatase inhibitors. The protein concentration was determined with a BCA kit, and then 5 × protein loading buffer was added to the sample, boiled at 100 °C for 10 min, and the sample was added to the SDS-PAGE gel sample hole for electrophoresis. After that, the proteins were transferred to the PVDF membrane. The membrane was blocked with 5 % skim milk at room temperature for 1 h and incubated with different antibodies (1:1000, Proteintech, China) at 4 °C overnight. The membrane was incubated with diluted secondary antibodies (1:5000, Proteintech, China) at room temperature for 1 h. Target protein visualization was carried out using an ECL working solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of mouse periodontitis model in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: All the animal experiments were conducted according to the ISO 10993-2:1992 animal welfare requirements and approved by the Medical Ethical Committee of School of Stomatology, Shandong University (Protocol Number: 20240333). Twelve hours before surgery, male C57BL/6 mice were subjected to fasting and water deprivation. Subsequently, they were anesthetized by inhalation of isoflurane. Specifically, a 5-0 non-absorbable silk thread was carefully wound around the cervix of the upper second molar for 10 d, with alternate-day injections of LPS. The silk thread was placed subgingivally and firmly tied. We ensured that the ligation was tight enough to cause local irritation and inflammation but not so tight as to damage the tooth or surrounding tissues. After the operation, the mice were closely monitored for their general condition, including food intake, activity level, and body weight. The silk thread was regularly checked to ensure it remained in place. The mice were then randomly divided into four groups (each with 6 mice): 1) Control group: received injections of 0.9 % sodium chloride solution only. 2) Periodontitis group: underwent ligature placement and local LPS injection. 3) Low-dose treatment group: received ligature placement and LPS injection, followed by every other day administration of carbon dots at a dose of 5 mg/mL, designated as “SeCDs-5″. 4) High-dose treatment group: underwent ligature placement and LPS injection, followed by every other day administration of SeCDs at a dose of 10 mg/mL, designated as ”SeCDs-10″.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-CT test and histological staining and analysis
ce:section.ce:para: After 2 or 4 weeks of treatment, the animal's maxilla and major organs were removed. Maxillas with teeth were collected, and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 1d, and the sample was then scanned with a micro-CT system. The 3D images of areas of interest (ROIs) were reassembled and analyzed. Bone parameters of ROIs were calculated and bone formation was quantitatively analyzed. Decalcification was performed at room temperature using 15 % EDTA for 21 d with changes of decalcification solution every 3 d. The samples were then processed for tissue dehydration, paraffin embedding, and sectioning (approximately 4 μm thick). Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and Masson's trichrome staining were conducted to assess periodontal inflammation and tissue morphology. Immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence techniques were employed to detect the expression of GPX4, SLC7A11, IL-6, and Runx2. All images were captured using a laser confocal microscope or Olympus optical microscope.

Data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (S.D.). Statistical comparisons were made by Student’s t-test between two groups or one-way ANOVA in three or more groups: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not significant; one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Dunnett’s multiple comparisons test.

SeCDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method using citric acid, curcumin, and L-selenocystine (Fig. 1 A). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed an excellent mono-dispersity and circular morphology of SeCDs (Fig. 1B). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image showed a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which matched the in-plane lattice spacing of (100) facets of graphene [29] (Fig. 1C). SeCDs possessed a uniformly sized morphology, with an average diameter of approximately 3.03 ± 0.04 nm (Fig. 1D). To unveil the surface functional groups of SeCDs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was applied (Fig. 1E). The broad peaks from 3500 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of O-H and N-H bonds (Fig. S1). Stretching and bending vibrations of C-H bonds were observed at 2950 and 1400 cm−1, respectively. The peaks at 1595, 1515, and 1407 cm−1 can be attributed to CO/CC, C–N, and C–O vibrations, respectively. Additional peaks from 800 cm−1 to 500 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of C-Se and Se-Se bonds [30]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of SeCDs showed two bands at around 284 and 306 nm, which were attributed to π-π transition of the CC bond and n-π transitions of CO bonds or other surface groups in conjugated sp2 domains, respectively (Fig. 1F). Under excitation at different wavelengths (350–420 nm), SeCDs showed strong blue fluorescence emission peaks at 426 nm, an excitation wavelength-dependent behavior and a certain degree of redshift (Fig. 1G), consistent with previous reports on CDs fluorescence imaging, possibly attributing to the size variations of carbon dots and the hole effects on the surface [31].

The components and chemical bonds of SeCDs were further analyzed. The XPS survey indicated that SeCDs were primarily composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and selenium, corresponding to characteristic peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Se 3d (Fig. S2). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks, corresponding to –C–C/CC (284.7 eV), −C–O/C–N/C–Se (286.1 eV), and –CO (288.2 eV) (Fig. 1H). The high-resolution N 1s spectrum exhibited three peaks located at 398.3, 399.5, and 400.7 eV, corresponding to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N [32] (Fig. 1I), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s was fitted by four types of oxygen bonds, which were assigned to −CO (531.3 eV), −C–OH (532.2 eV), −C–O–C (532.9 eV), and −COOH (533.8 eV) (Fig. 1J). Finally, the deconvoluted Se 3d (55.8 eV) spectrum revealed the presence of –C–Se–C units [33] (Fig. 1K). The above results confirmed that selenium was embedded into the SeCDs structure, and the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyls and carboxylates imparted the SeCDs with good water dispersibility.

The chelation between CDs and metal ions usually causes the fluorescence quenching of CDs. This property was used to detect the ability of SeCDs to scavenge Fe3+ and Fe2+ [34]. After mixing with Fe3+ or Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity sharply decreased, especially for Fe3+, which exhibited a concentration-dependent effect (Fig. 1L and Fig. S3). Notably, the two most abundant bioactive divalent cations in the body, Ca2+ and Mg2+ did not affect the fluorescence intensity, confirming that SeCDs do not interfere with the normal biological processes regulated by Ca2+ and Mg2+, thus ensuring good biosecurity. Additionally, the transparent solution of SeCDs gradually turned turbid after combining with the Fe3+ solution, accompanied by a large amount of brown precipitation (Fig. S4). The formed mixture of SeCDs and Fe3+ showed an irregular self-assembled structure, which was completely different from the original monodisperse circular shape of SeCDs (Fig. 1M). Elemental mapping analysis further confirmed the capacity of SeCDs to bind Fe3+, likely attributed to the coordination function introduced by curcumin [35]. Meanwhile, the high-resolution XPS spectra showed that the main peaks around 711.3 and 725.4 eV in the SeCDs/Fe3+ complex were assigned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, and the content of Fe3+ reached 11.7 % (w/w %) (Fig. S5). Efficient chelation of metal ions in vitro indicated that SeCDs were capable of binding excessive iron ions in the extracellular tissue environment, thereby reducing excessive deposition of iron ions in the different organs. This extracellular ion chelation holds significant importance for safeguarding the normal physiological functions of intracellular compartments, particularly crucial organelles [36]. Moreover, varied pH almost did not change the fluorescence intensity, indicating the excellent chemical stability of SeCDs and their robust tolerance to acidic and basic environments (Fig. S6). We further discussed the degradation of SeCDs in vitro. As depicted in Fig. S7, the yellow carbon dot solution gradually faded after undergoing a reaction for 48 h in an oxidative stress environment simulated by H2O2. At the same time, the specific fluorescence intensity at 426 nm steadily declined, suggesting that SeCDs were being gradually decomposed. According to our previous studies, CDs can be degraded by ROS [37,38], also confirmed by the ROS scavenging ability of CDs. Free radicals can assail CDs in multiple ways (such as transferring electrons to CDs, extracting H atoms from CDs, and hydroxylating CDs by attacking the CC bond of CDs), thereby leading to the degradation of CDs [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS-scavenging ability and biocompatibility of SeCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxyl radicals were crucial reactive oxygen species. In cases of cellular iron overload, accelerated Fenton reactions led to hydroxyl radical bursts, DNA strand breaks, and unsaturated fatty acid peroxidation. Previous reports have indicated that Se-doped nanoparticles have excellent antioxidant properties and biocompatibility [40]. To assess the free radical scavenging ability of the synthesized SeCDs, the scavenging levels of •OH and DPPH were tested. First of all, •OH was detected by the change of color of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) at 652 nm, which was oxidized by •OH to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) [41]. Sixty% of •OH was scavenged in the presence of SeCDs at the concentration of 100 μg/mL (Fig. 2 A-B). Meanwhile, SeCDs also presented a similar concentration-dependent manner for the scavenging of reactive nitrogen species (RNS) by using 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl radical (DPPH) as an indicator (Fig. 2C-D). The biocompatibility of SeCDs was verified by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assay and Live/Dead staining. The results indicated that when the concentration was below 10 μg/mL, SeCDs showed good biocompatibility to both GFs and PDLSCs (Fig. 2E-G and Fig. S8), which guided the subsequent concentration setting. The protective effect of SeCDs on GFs and PDLSCs under oxidative stress induced by H2O2 was observed using a 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) fluorescence probe. The presence of SeCDs significantly eliminated intracellular ROS, demonstrating the ability to counter intracellular oxidative stresses as an excellent free radical scavenger (Fig. 2H-I and Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SeCDs inhibition of ferroptosis by multi-hierarchy iron scavenging
ce:section.ce:para: Iron is a vital element for living organisms, playing a crucial role in ATP production, DNA synthesis, and repair. However, excessive iron can cause cell damage and even programmed cell death-ferroptosis [35] (Fig. 3 A). The internalization and intracellular distribution of SeCDs were first assessed to show the potential of SeCDs for the chelation of cytoplasmic and mitochondrial iron. As shown in Fig. 3B, the blue fluorescence showed good internalization of SeCDs, and the colocalization of green fluorescent lyso-tracker and blue fluorescent SeCDs demonstrated that part of SeCDs were partly internalized by lysosome-mediated endocytosis (Fig. 3B, D). Interestingly, SeCDs were enriched in mitochondria based on the colocalization of red fluorescent mito-tracker and blue fluorescent SeCDs (Fig. 3C, E). Studies have demonstrated the surface functional groups and charge properties of CDs can influence lysosomal escape and mitochondria enrichment. SeCDs rich in amino groups had positive zeta potential, especially under the acidic conditions of the lysosomes (4.4 mV, pH = 7.4; 8.7 mV, pH = 5.0). Therefore, SeCDs can effectively escape from lysosomes due to the protonation of the amino groups at low pH [42,43]. Further, the abundant amino groups on the surface and positive potential also endowed SeCDs to concentrate around mitochondria through electrostatic attraction-mediated passive diffusion [44], providing the intrinsic advantage of chelating excessive iron in mitochondria.

Next, the efficiency of ferroptosis inhibition was further confirmed. As a ferroptosis inducer, ferric ammonium citrate (FAC) was used to provide an exogenous iron source to simulate a typical iron-induced ferroptosis model [20], and it was found that the ferroptosis-suppressed genes showed low expression in a FAC concentration-dependent manner (Fig. S10). In addition, FerroOrange as a fluorescence probe was utilized to measure the intracellular free iron content. Compared with the control group, a distinct brightening of yellow fluorescence was observed in the FAC group. Whereas, the fluorescence intensity in the SeCDs group was notably decreased, which could be attributed to a reduction of the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in the cytoplasm and the chelation of the latter (Fig. 3F-G). For instance, the clinically used iron chelator deferoxamine (DFO) also showed a high affinity for Fe3+ and a low affinity for Fe2+. DFO mainly forms complexes with Fe3+ and it is excreted from urine and feces to prevent iron overload and iron deposition in various organs. As depicted in Fig. 3H-K, SeCDs dramatically decreased the production of MDA (lipid peroxidation marker) and increased the differential expression of ferroptosis-related genes (GPX4, Solute Carrier Family 7 Member 11/SLC7A11). Acyl-CoA Synthetase Long-Chain Family Member 4 (ACSL4) was a lipid metabolic enzyme that enhanced lipid peroxide generation by promoting the esterification of PUFAs into acyl-CoA and thereby facilitating ferroptosis. Knocking out ACSL4 or reducing its expression can significantly improve the ferroptotic state of cells [4]. The excellent iron chelating effect of SeCDs in the cytoplasm was beneficial for the regulation of phospholipid hydroperoxides (PLOOHs, a lipid-based form of reactive oxygen species). As the executors of ferroptosis, PLOOHs mainly reacted with Fe2+ and Fe3+ to generate free radicals PLO• and PLOO• respectively. Subsequently, these free radicals reacted with polyunsaturated fatty acid phospholipids (PUFA-PLs) to further propagate PLOOH production. The chelation of iron in the cytoplasm can inhibit the vicious cycle of PLOOH generation and the initiation of ferroptosis. Furthermore, SeCDs were able to decrease mitochondrial iron, as confirmed by the fluorescence imaging of the targeted mitochondrial iron probe (Fig. 3L). The iron content in mitochondria accounts for about 20 % to 50 % of the total cellular iron. It primarily functions as a cofactor for iron-containing proteins to participate in the electron transfer of enzymatic redox reactions. The imbalance of iron metabolism in mitochondria leads to iron overload, which in turn initiates oxidation reactions with lipids and DNA in mitochondria, thereby destroying the structure and function of mitochondria and exacerbating the ferroptosis process [45]. Therefore, the mitochondrial aggregation effect of SeCDs will enhance the regulation of mitochondrial iron homeostasis and ferroptosis inhibition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SeCDs inhibition of ferroptosis under infection
ce:section.ce:para: During a pathogen infection and an inflammatory response, the cellular iron homeostasis is disrupted. Several inflammatory factors upregulate hepcidin expression via the JAK2/STAT3 pathway. Excessive hepcidin leads to intracellular iron retention, thereby impacting immune cell regulation [46]. Therefore, inflammation and iron overload form a mutually reinforcing adverse cycle. To mimic an infectious inflammation microenvironment, the model of LPS-induced ferroptosis was developed. After LPS stimulation, the level of intracellular iron increased consequently, consistent with a previous report [47] (Fig. 4 A and Fig. S11). During ferroptosis, the inactivated redox system will result in mitochondrial atrophy and enhanced lipid peroxidation [48]. C11/BODIPY lipid probe was used to identify the amount of lipid ROS. The LPS-treated GFs exhibited the enhanced green fluorescence of oxidized C11/BODIPY. However, after the treatment with SeCDs, the content of N-oxidized C11/BODIPY increased, indicating the inhibition of lipid peroxidation level (Fig. 4B-C and Fig. S12). Concomitantly, SeCDs promoted the expression of ferroptosis inhibitory genes (GPX4, SLC7A11, FTH1, and Nrf2) that are decreased by LPS (Fig. 4D). Moreover, the immunofluorescence staining of GPX4 also demonstrated the inhibition effect on ferroptosis caused by infection (Fig. 4E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Restoring mitochondrial homeostasis enhances the inhibition of ferroptosis
ce:section.ce:para: Mitochondrial structural and functional impairments have been used as classic markers of ferroptosis [49]. To clarify the relation between mitochondrial homeostasis and ferroptosis, mtROS level, MMP, and the state of mPTP were characterized. Studies have shown that mitochondrial reactive oxygen species (mtROS) play a pivotal role in diacyl-PUFA phosphatidylcholines (PC-PUFA2s)-induced ferroptosis. PC-PUFA2s induce an increase in mitochondrial superoxide levels, likely by disrupting the structure of complex I and altering electron flow. Administration of the mitochondria-targeted antioxidant MitoQ substantially mitigates the accumulation of mtROS triggered by PC-PUFA2s and prevents cell death [48]. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the level of mtROS significantly increased as a result of LPS-induced iron overload. In contrast, SeCDs inhibited the generation of mtROS on account of their antioxidant capacity and iron chelation effect. Given that the decrease in electric potential energy serves as an early indicator of mitochondrial damage, MMP was assessed using JC-1 staining. The red-green fluorescence ratio decreased in the LPS group, while SeCDs downregulated the generation of JC-1 monomers, underscoring its function in effectively increasing MMP (Fig. 5B-C). The closure or opening of mPTP is of crucial significance for mitochondrial function. Transient mPTP opening in subconductance states may have multiple physiological effects, such as changes in mitochondrial bioenergetics and rapid Ca2+ efflux. However, prolonged opening of the mPTP can lead to mitochondrial swelling and the rupture of the mitochondrial outer membrane. mPTP fluorescence detection was employed to ascertain the mPTP status (Fig. 5D-E). The reduced fluorescence signal in the LPS group indicated a sustained opening of the mPTP, while SeCDs attenuated the degree of mPTP opening, which alleviated the damage to mitochondrial respiratory chain function, mitochondrial expansion, and ROS accumulation.

Furthermore, the specific structural changes in mitochondria were characterized visually through TEM (Fig. 5F). After LPS stimulation, the mitochondria underwent shrinkage, reduction or even disappearance of cristae, and increased membrane density, which was consistent with the morphological changes observed during ferroptosis [50], while the normal morphology of mitochondria was instead restored after SeCDs treatment. ATP synthesis stands as a robust indicator to assess mitochondrial function. Following treatment with SeCDs, the reduced ATP synthesis caused by LPS was also restored to nearly normal levels, indicating that SeCDs were able to enhance cellular energy metabolism (Fig. 5G). As an important component of the intracellular antioxidant system, reduced glutathione (GSH) acts as an auxiliary factor for GPX4 activity to protect mitochondria from oxidative damage. As illustrated in Fig. 5H, LPS decreased the intracellular GSH/GSSG ratio and disrupted the cell's intrinsic antioxidant system. However, SeCDs displayed a remedial and salvaging function. In addition, the Mito-tracker and Mito-FerroGreen dyes showed that SeCDs reduced the level of mitochondrial iron and increased the number of mitochondria (Fig. 5I), meaning that the SeCDs can promote mitochondrial biogenesis and further promoted mitochondrial function [51]. Therefore, SeCDs can regulate mitochondrial homeostasis by decreasing the mitochondrial iron level, scavenging mtROS, increasing MMP, inhibiting mPTP excessive opening, and promoting the mitochondrial generation, following to restoration of ATP content and GSH/GSSG balance (Fig. 5J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: SeCDs inhibition of inflammation and promotion of osteogenic differentiation
ce:section.ce:para: It is now widely recognized that ferroptosis and inflammation mutually exacerbate [52]. During lipid peroxidation, polyunsaturated fatty acids, especially arachidonic acid (AA), undergo sustained metabolism catalyzed by lipoxygenases, promoting the secretion of inflammatory signals. Intervention with ferroptosis inhibitors or iron chelators can significantly suppress the expression of inflammatory genes. Ferrostatin (Fer) was utilized as a ferroptosis inhibitor to assess and compare the inhibitory effects of SeCDs on ferroptosis and inflammation. As shown in Fig. 6 A, Fer and SeCDs both reduced the expression of inflammatory genes. Immunofluorescence analysis also revealed that IL-6 expression decreased after SeCDs treatment (Fig. 6B), suggesting that SeCDs can combat inflammation. Furthermore, studies have demonstrated that the accumulation of iron in the tissue microenvironment can lead to the apoptosis of osteoblasts, and enhance the differentiation ability of osteoclasts [53,54]. As illustrated in Fig. 6C, LPS inhibited osteogenesis-related gene expression (ALP, Runx2). ALP staining also showed the osteogenic function of PDLSCs in an inflammatory environment was damaged (Fig. 6D). However, the osteogenic differentiation efficacy after administration of SeCDs was even higher than that in LPS group. Similarly, immunofluorescence staining of OCN and Alizarin Red S Staining (ARS) both showed a similar trend (Fig. 6E-F). Therefore, SeCDs can restore and further promote the osteogenic differentiation of stem cells under ferroptosis and inflammatory environments, showing an attractive potential for inflammation control and tissue repair. In addition, iron metabolism disorders have an impact on immune regulation, such as macrophage polarization [55]. Macrophage reprogramming and the balance of osteoblast/osteoclast-mediated bone remodeling are constrained by iron overload and ferroptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The underlying mechanism of SeCDs as a ferroptosis inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: To further elucidate the specific mechanism of SeCDs inhibiting ferroptosis, a non-targeted lipid metabolomic analysis was conducted. Based on the criteria of variable importance projection (VIP) > 1 and P value < 0.05, a total of 35 significantly altered metabolites (Fig. S13) were successfully screened, of which 12 metabolites had increased expression and 23 metabolites had decreased expression in the treatment group (Fig. 7 A, B). These metabolites included fatty acids (such as linoleic acid and arachidonic acid/AA), glycerolipids (such as triglyceride/TG and diglyceride/DG), glycerophospholipids (such as phosphatidylcholine/PC, phosphatidylethanolamine/PE, and phosphatidic acid/PA), and sphingolipids (such as ceramide/Cer and sphingomyelin/SM) (Fig. S14). SM possesses anti-inflammatory effects and a decrease in SM metabolism can worsen acute inflammation [56]. Within the SeCDs group, the observed upregulation of SM expression implied that SeCDs may exert anti-inflammatory effects to a certain extent possibly by promoting SM metabolism. Additionally, lysophosphatidylcholine (LPC) has been proven to aggravate eosinophil infiltration, lung function impairment, and airway inflammatory diseases (especially asthma) [57]. The decreased LPC levels in our experiment suggested that inhibiting LPC metabolism may play a beneficial role in suppressing inflammation.

Not all membrane-localized lipids containing PUFA contribute to ferroptosis. Specifically, phosphatidylethanolamines (PEs) with AA tail (PE-AA, 20:4) or adrenic acid (AdA) tail (PE-AdA, 22:4) were identified as the primary PUFA-PLs undergoing peroxidation reaction during ferroptosis, which was consistent with a previous study [58]. Fig. 7C illustrates the top 10 upregulated and downregulated metabolites with the highest VIP values, while Fig. 7D indicated that LPC (16:0) and LPC (18:0) exhibited the highest positive correlation coefficient, whereas PS (40:1) and PE (16:0p/20:5) showed the highest negative correlation coefficient. Metabolic pathway and differential metabolite heatmap analyses demonstrated significant alterations in glycerophospholipid metabolism and unsaturated fatty acid metabolism in the treatment group (Fig. 7E). It has already demonstrated the crucial role of glycerophospholipid metabolic pathways in governing the pluripotency and iron homeostasis of human pluripotent stem cells while correlating with cellular processes such as proliferation, differentiation, and apoptosis. Alterations in glycerophospholipid proportions can serve as indicators of disrupted lipid metabolism. Initially, previous results suggested that AA or AdA were the primary substrates for phospholipid/lipid peroxidation in ferroptosis. However, recent data indicated a broad range of PUFAs, such as linoleic acid and α-linolenic acid, which can be elongated and desaturated in cells to produce longer and more unsaturated PUFAs (Figs. S15–S17). Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) further confirmed the significant inhibition of arachidonic acid metabolism, linoleic acid metabolism, glycerophospholipid metabolism, and α-linolenic acid metabolism in the SeCDs group (Fig. 7F-H and Fig. S18). Furthermore, studies have shown that the AMPK-ACC-PUFAs pathway plays an important role in regulating the synthesis of PUFAs during ferroptosis. Energy stress increases the phosphorylation of AMPK substrate acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC). AMPK-mediated phosphorylation of ACC inhibits fatty acid synthesis and resists ferroptosis under glucose starvation conditions [59]. Western blot results revealed that within the LPS group, the expression of phosphorylated AMPK (p-AMPK) and phosphorylated acetyl-CoA carboxylase (p-ACC) remarkably decreased compared with AMPK and ACC (Fig. 7I). On the contrary, the phosphorylation ratio of the SeCDs group was recovered. The results suggested that SeCDs activated AMPK and facilitated the phosphorylation of substrate protein ACC, thereby hindering the synthesis of PUFAs and preventing ferroptosis (Fig. 7J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The comprehensive therapeutic effect of SeCDs on periodontitis
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the in vivo therapeutic efficiency of SeCDs, periodontitis was selected as a representative model. Periodontitis is a chronic infectious disease related to systemic diseases, with a high prevalence rate and a significant impact on human health [60]. Meanwhile, ferroptosis is closely related to periodontitis [61], and even a classification model and an immune-related network based on ferroptosis in periodontitis has been developed [62]. The periodontitis model was constructed in C57BL/6 mice using a ligature placement in conjunction with local injection of LPS, as illustrated in Fig. 8 A.

We assessed the levels of ferroptosis-related proteins (GPX4, SLC7A11) to investigate the inhibitory effects of SeCDs on ferroptosis and inflammation in vivo. As a key molecule in inhibiting ferroptosis, GPX4 can catalytically reduce lipid ROS to harmless alcohols with the assistance of the antioxidant GSH. SLC7A11, as a transmembrane protein that plays a transport role on System Xc-, exhibits decreased expression in the ferroptosis environment. Compared to the control group, gingival tissues slice from the periodontitis group showed a decreased GPX4 and SLC7A11 expression (Fig. 8B-E and Fig. S19). But SeCDs group presented the overexpression of both GPX4 and SLC7A11 and mitigated tissue damage. This proved the positive role of SeCDs' multi-level iron chelation in inhibiting ferroptosis. Furthermore, the typical inflammatory indicator IL-6 decreased after SeCDs administration, also proving an efficient anti-inflammatory effect in vivo (Fig. 8F and Fig. S20).

Given that alveolar bone (AB) resorption represented a hallmark of periodontitis, micro-CT analysis was used to evaluate the bone mass after treatment with SeCDs. As shown in Fig. 9 A, compared to the pronounced bone resorption and evident root exposure observed in the periodontitis group, the local application of SeCDs within the gingival sulcus reduced alveolar bone resorption and the distance between ABC and CEJ after two weeks (Fig. S21). By the fourth week, the bone level basically returned to the normal level. Noteworthy, the SeCDs group exhibited elevated bone parameters such as bone volume/total volume (BV/TV) and reduced trabecular separation (Tb.Sp) (Fig. 9B). In addition, histological examination of periodontal tissue sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and Masson's trichrome revealed the disrupted periodontal supporting tissue architecture, decreased collagen content, and increased inflammatory response in the diseased group (Fig. 9C-D). However, the SeCDs group was found to promote collagen deposition and inhibit inflammatory cell recruitment, thereby improving the inflammatory microenvironment and halting the progression of periodontitis. Furthermore, we observed an increased expression of the osteogenesis-related transcription factor 2 (Runx2) in the SeCDs group compared to the periodontitis group, which bore significant implications for periodontal bone regeneration (Fig. 9E and Fig. S22). Dose control is crucial to avoid excessive chelation of iron and biosecurity. Meanwhile, cells have a certain degree of adaptability and regulatory ability to maintain cellular iron homeostasis. Admittedly, when there is an excessive dose of iron chelators, there is a possibility of causing significant damage to cellular iron homeostasis. This, in turn, impacts the normal function and survival of the cells. Biochemical analyses of serum parameters following SeCDs treatment compared to the control group revealed no significant alterations, including liver function indicators (AST, ALT) and renal function indicators (CRE, BUN) (Fig. S23), suggesting negligible hepatotoxicity and renal toxicity by SeCDs. Likewise, blood parameters also remained within normal ranges. As indicated by H&E staining, no significant pathological changes were observed in major organs after SeCDs treatment (Fig. S24). These findings preliminarily supported that the used dose of SeCDs in vivo was considered safe, meaning that it would not disrupt the cell's normal physiological functions (such as osteogenic differentiation). Therefore, SeCDs can be considered as promising nanomedicines for promoting tissue repair and treating periodontitis by inhibiting ferroptosis, suppressing inflammation, and enhancing osteogenic differentiation.

In summary, SeCDs were successfully fabricated by a single-step reaction and presented good iron chelation and ROS scavenging abilities. SeCDs can be efficiently internalized into cells and were especially enriched in mitochondria. Iron overload was significantly inhibited by extracellular/cytoplasmic/mitochondrial multi-hierarchy iron chelation. By activating the AMPK pathway and increasing selenoprotein GPX4 level, SeCDs could suppress the metabolism of glycerophospholipids/polyunsaturated fatty acids and lipid peroxidation. Moreover, SeCDs could restore mitochondrial homeostasis by chelating mitochondrial iron, scavenging mtROS, and increasing GPX4 levels, assisting in ferroptosis suppression. Finally, SeCDs rescued the osteogenic differentiation potential of stem cells, which was weakened by ferroptosis. In vivo experiments proved that SeCDs can significantly inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation, and facilitate tissue regeneration. Overall, a multifunctional nanosystem was constructed to inhibit ferroptosis and inflammation by multi-hierarchy iron chelation and regulation of mitochondrial homeostasis.
